headline: CATTLE ON FEED DROPS 10%; BEANS AND WHEAT SELL OFF
subheadline: Live cattle ran 2.3% on bullish Cattle on Feed data while soybeans dropped 24¾ cents and wheat gave back 14 cents heading into a 3-day weekend.
lead: The USDA Cattle on Feed report, out at 2:00 PM CT today, put May placements down 10% from last year, and live cattle answered at $254.80, up 2.3%, the complex's biggest single-session gain in weeks. Meanwhile, soybeans fell 24¾ cents to $11.23 and wheat dropped 14 cents to $6.06, both running into pre-Juneteenth weekend position-squaring with no new demand catalyst to hold the line. The week that began with a China purchase story and a wheat run ended with the grain complex fading and cattle doing the real work.
the_takeaway: Cattle's structural tightness is real; grains need a new demand catalyst to stop the bleed.
teaser: Cattle on Feed placements -10% does the heavy lifting while beans and wheat sell into the long weekend.

one_number.unit: year-over-year drop in May cattle placements
one_number.context: USDA's Cattle on Feed report showed May placements fell 10% from last year, a number that tells you the pipeline is tightening, not just the processing side. Fewer cattle placed now means fewer cattle coming to market this fall and winter. Live cattle at $254.80 isn't just chart noise; it's a supply story the placement number just confirmed.

yesterdays_call.summary: Old-crop soybean sellers: if exports top 400K MT the nearby cushion firms; under 300K MT, the oil break pulls beans lower.
yesterdays_call.note: Beans dropped 24¾ cents today; the export total and oil break pulled the trigger exactly as called.

sections.title: CATTLE ON FEED DELIVERS
sections.body: Live cattle ran to **$254.80**, up 2.3% on the session, the biggest single-day gain the complex has put up in weeks, and the USDA Cattle on Feed report at 2:00 PM CT gave it the foundation. May placements dropped 10% from last year, according to University of Tennessee ag economist Charley Martinez, a reflection of tight feeder supplies and the processing-constrained environment the ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan lockout has created. Fewer cattle going into feedlots now means a tighter fed cattle market later in the year. Feeders, still feeling the supply-side squeeze from the lockout, held nearly flat at **$366.60**, down just 0.2%, which is about as close to unchanged as the complex gets. The live/feeder spread is talking: the fed cattle market is pricing in forward tightness that the feeder market already knew.
sections.catalyst: USDA Cattle on Feed: May placements down 10% year-over-year, confirming structural supply tightness.
sections.bottom_line: Placements down 10% is a supply story, not a chart story. Fall cattle are getting more valuable by the report.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: narrative held and then some; placement data confirmed the structural thesis.

sections.title: SOYBEANS SLIDE INTO WEEKEND
sections.body: Nearby soybeans fell **24¾ cents** to $11.23, a 2.2% drop that pulled the rest of the oilseed complex with it. Thursday's export sales total came in light enough that the cushion from this week's China soybean purchases couldn't hold the market up into a 3-day weekend. Soybean oil added another leg lower, settling at **$69.69**, down 1.7%, two days after its 6.7% break, and meal fell to **$301.30**, down 1.1%. The pattern this week: China came back to the U.S. soybean market, per Ag Bull Trading's Tommy Grisafi, but tariff structure and the 20% effective discount already priced into grains made it more of a relief rally than a fundamental reset. New crop November beans held better at **$11.43**, off only 4¾ cents, and that spread tells you: traders see the nearby as rich relative to what production looks like.
sections.catalyst: Pre-holiday position-squaring after light export sales; soybean oil two-day break dragging crush margins lower.
sections.bottom_line: China buying is real but not enough to hold beans into a long weekend without better export volume.
sections.vs_yesterday: Soybeans: the oil break pulled beans exactly as the call warned; cushion gone.

sections.title: WHEAT RETREATS AFTER BIG WEEK
sections.body: Chicago wheat gave back **14 cents** to $6.06, a 2.3% drop on a day when the news bucket had nothing new to add. This week's story for wheat was Monday's 4.2% run on weather premium, then two sessions of slowly unwinding that premium as the Corn Belt's unseasonably cool, wet pattern ahead actually looks constructive for growing conditions, not damaging. Brownfield's weather desk puts near- to below-normal temps across most of the Heartland for the 6-to-10 day period, which is not the drought premium setup that drives wheat to new highs. At $6.06, wheat is still well above where it started the week; today is giveback, not breakdown. But the funds that ran into wheat on Monday's weather story are stepping out ahead of the weekend, and there's no fresh catalyst to keep them in.
sections.catalyst: Cool, wet 6-10 day Heartland forecast removes weather premium; pre-holiday fund liquidation.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat's weekly gain survives; today's retreat is position management, not a trend reversal.

sections.title: CORN DRIFTS, ENERGY FIRMS
sections.body: Corn eased 2¼ cents to **$4.17** on nearby, with December off 3¼ cents to **$4.44**. A new study from U.S. Farmers and Ranchers in Action warns that without expanded biofuels markets, U.S. corn could lose roughly 30 million acres by 2050; that's a long-horizon number, not today's catalyst, but it's the structural argument for why ethanol policy matters to basis. Today, the corn market is a quiet follower of the soybean slide. WTI crude held at **$75.76**, up 1.6%, a modest rebound after the energy complex spent the week pricing in return of Iranian supply. The Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, drove crude down nearly 9% for the week, per oilprice.com, but today's session shows the sellers taking a breath. Natural gas added 1.5% to **$3.20**, consistent with summer cooling demand starting to assert itself.
sections.catalyst: Corn follows bean complex lower; WTI bounces modestly as Iran supply-return fears ease but don't vanish.
sections.bottom_line: Corn is a passenger today; ethanol policy is the long-run floor the market isn't pricing yet.

sections.title: METALS BREAK HARD
sections.body: Gold fell **4.0%** to **$4,167** and silver dropped **6.5%** to **$64.77**, both putting up their second significant down session this week. The dollar index running to **$100.79**, up 0.6%, is part of the story; a stronger dollar reprices safe-haven metals lower. But there's a macro read here that matters for ag input costs: when gold and silver fall this hard into a risk-on equity session (S&P at **$7,500.58**, up 1.1%), the money is rotating toward equities and away from inflation hedges. That rotation tells you the market is buying the Iran peace framework as real, not just a headline. For producers, the input cost implication is that the geopolitical risk premium in energy and specialty inputs has room to keep unwinding.
sections.catalyst: Dollar index up 0.6% and Iran diplomatic progress driving safe-haven liquidation across precious metals.
sections.bottom_line: Metals breaking this hard into an equity rally says the risk-off trade is unwinding, not just pausing.

outside_the_pit.title: Hormuz Traffic Stalls as U.S.-Iran Talks Show Friction
outside_the_pit.body: A day after a rush of tankers moved through the Strait of Hormuz on diplomatic optimism, tanker traffic is pulling back as shippers reassess. The Iran-Hormuz premium isn't gone; it's uneven. Crude's weekly 9% decline may have priced in more certainty than the tanker lanes are actually delivering.

outside_the_pit.title: 30 Million Corn Acres at Risk Without Expanded Biofuels Policy
outside_the_pit.body: A U.S. Farmers and Ranchers in Action study projects the U.S. could lose roughly 30 million corn acres by 2050 if biofuels demand doesn't grow. That's a 2050 headline, but it's the political argument for Renewable Fuel Standard expansion that corn producers will be making to Washington all year.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm Perspectives from Both Sides of the Border
outside_the_pit.body: Farm Press is hosting Texas and Mexico ranchers for a ground-level screwworm update. With confirmed cases still active in U.S. livestock, the livestock disease calculus hasn't been resolved by diplomacy the way energy markets have been. Watch the cattle complex for any escalation in case counts.

spread_to_watch.label: Nearby soybeans / November soybeans inverse
spread_to_watch.level: 20-cent carry, new crop at $11.43 vs old crop $11.23, narrowing
spread_to_watch.commentary: Old crop fell 24¾ cents today while new crop gave back only 4¾ cents; the spread is telling you traders see nearby beans as the risk position, not the value. If China buying accelerates into old-crop pipeline, that spread compresses fast. If Thursday's export pace holds light, new crop stays the cleaner hold.

basis.headline: Corn basis soft nationwide; soy basis widening into weekend.
basis.body: **Corn basis is running soft across the Belt**, consistent with adequate on-farm storage and no urgent commercial demand pulling bushels. Eastern Belt ethanol grind is steady but not aggressive enough to move basis. **Soybean basis is widening** into the long weekend as crushers step back ahead of the holiday and no flash-export demand is on the board to tighten it. Producers with old-crop soybeans in storage should watch Monday's reopening closely: if China reappears with flash sale volume, basis moves fast.

weekly_thread.question: Will USDA Crop Progress confirm the Belt caught up to seasonal pace after May's wet start?
weekly_thread.status_text: The 2026 planting season caught up and then handed the baton to growing conditions: Monday's Crop Progress confirmed the Belt is on schedule, and this week's weather story shifted to a cool, wet 6-to-10 day pattern that keeps soil moisture adequate but weather premium off the table for now. The season's big question answered; the next one is whether that moisture holds through pollination.

the_more_you_know.title: Placement Numbers: The Cattle Market's Six-Month Time Machine
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 10% drop in May placements isn't just a today story; it's a fall and winter cattle market story. When a steer goes into a feedlot today, it comes out roughly 150 to 180 days later. That puts today's placement shortfall squarely in the November-through-January fed cattle pipeline. The Cargill Fort Morgan lockout removed roughly 2% of weekly slaughter capacity and constrained where cattle could go; that's already in the feedlot math. What today's number adds is that even before the lockout, fewer cattle were moving into placements. Two constraints on the same pipeline compound. Producers with fall cattle on feed are sitting on an asset the USDA just told you is getting scarcer.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: watch the good-to-excellent rating for corn; above 75% nationally keeps weather premium off the table, below 70% starts the pollination-risk conversation.
Weekly Export Sales: soybeans need 400K MT or better to signal China's return is sustained, not a one-week flush. Under 300K MT and November beans test $11.30.
USDA Acreage Report: the season's make-or-break for corn and soybean planted area; a surprise in either direction moves the market hard. Get positions set before the weekend before.

Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic: if passage normalizes five or more carriers per day, the residual geopolitical premium in crude finishes unwinding. If traffic stalls, crude's Friday bounce was a floor, not a ceiling.

Cargill Fort Morgan lockout resolution: no announcement as of today. Every week of continued lockout tightens the fall cattle pipeline. Watch for any labor news that changes the 6,000-head daily processing shortfall.

daily_quote.text: Fool me once, shame on you; fool me twice, shame on me.

source_summary: USDA Cattle on Feed (June 2026); Brownfield Ag News; oilprice.com; farmpolicynews.illinois.edu; feedstuffs.com; AGSIST LOCKED PRICE TABLE June 19, 2026
